pages.text: Freeze–thaw cycle test and damage mechanics models of alkali-activated slag concrete Yawei Fu ⇑, Liangcai Cai, Yonggen Wu College of Engineering, Air Force Engineering University, Xi’an 710038, China article info Article history: Received 12 October 2010 Received in revised form 5 December 2010 Accepted 7 December 2010 Available online 18 February 2011 Keywords: Alkali-activated slag concrete Freeze–thaw cycle Microstructure Dynamic elasticity modulus Damage mechanics models abstract Alkali-activated slag concrete (ASC) was prepared by using slag and Na 2SiO3, NaOH complex activator. Freeze–thaw resistance, microstructure, performance mechanism, inner freeze–thaw damage distribu- tion and mechanics models of ASC were investigated by freeze–thaw cycle, SEM and EDS tests. The exper- imental results show that ASC has excellent freeze–thaw resistance with frost-resisting grade of F300 at lowest; hydration products of ASC are mostly C–S–H(I) with low Ca/Si, alkaline aluminosilicate and zeo- lite mineral, it does not have Ca(OH) 2 and transition strip, compact degree and uniformity of structure are better, and its high compressive strength (90 MPa) also makes high freeze–thaw resistance, therefore, ASC has excellent freeze–thaw durability; freeze–thaw damage mechanics models of ASC were estab- lished by using relative dynamic elasticity modulus, dynamic elasticity modulus attenuation model is superior to accumulative freeze–thaw damage model, power function model is superior to exponential function model with better precision and relativity, and the models can reﬂect freeze–thaw damage rule and degree accurately. Crown Copyright /C2112010 Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction In recent years, there are plentiful studies on a new green bind- ing material—alkali-activated cement, it can be prepared by wastes containing kaolinite or aluminosilicate (such as kaolinite ore, coal gangue, slag or ﬂy ash) and chemic activator. Under a strongly alkaline solution, –O–Si–O–Al–O– in vitreous body structure is rap- idly dissolved into solution to form [SiO 4]4/C0 and [AlO 4]5/C0 tetrahe- dral units, then new –O–Si–O–Al–O– binding materials with three-dimensional network structure are yield by shrinking and polymerization reaction. With the arriving of low carbon economy time, international governments attach more importance to energy saving, emission reducing and cycled economics. The production of alkali-activated cement requires much lower calcining temperature (600–800 /C176C), consumes 70% less energy sources and emits 80%–90% less CO 2 than Portland cement (PC), so it can be called a genuine low carbon cement. Nowadays, basal properties and freeze–thaw resistance of alkali-activated cement concrete have been systematically studied [1–12] . However, little systems analysis had been done in the relation between its freeze–thaw resistance and microstructure, especially void of work had been done in its freeze–thaw damage rules and mechanics models. And in cold area, freeze–thaw damage is the primary factor to the depressing of performance of concrete structure, which can threaten the durability and safety of concrete structure, thus academe and engineer have attach more importance to it. As the essential development and important supplement of fracture mechanics, damage mechanics is the part and parcel of material structure distortion and wreck theory, it emphasizes on the inﬂuence of material damage to macroscopical mechanics

2. Freeze–thaw cycle experimentation 2.1. Materials Slag used in this study was metallurgy blast furnace slag, was supplied by Jiang- xi Building Materials Plant, PR China, its speciﬁc surface is 410 m 2/kg, chemical compositions of slag are listed in Table 1 . NaOH and Na 2SiO3 sodium silicate multiplex solution was used as alkali activa- tor, module of sodium silicate is 3.34. Sand with ﬁneness modulus of 2.78 was used as ﬁne aggregate. Limestone were used as crushed rock aggregate (5–20 mm:20– 40 mm = 45:55). 2.2. Mix proportion and specimen preparation Mix proportion, workability and compressive strength at 28 d of ASC are listed in Table 2. It was prepared by a single decubital axis compellent beater with content of 60 L. Firstly, NaOH and Na 2SiO3 were dissolved in water to form alkali activator, then slag and activator were mixed, and slag granules dispersed in the solution to form binding material, materials adding order and whisking time were: sand + slag (30 s) ? stone(30 s) ? activator solution (120 s). Next, fresh concrete was poured into steel moulds with 100 mm /C2 100 mm /C2 400 mm for freeze–thaw cycle test and damage mechanics analysis, vibrating for 60–90 s. After 24 h, the samples were demoulded and cured under scheduled regimes. Thirty samples were tested for freeze–thaw cycle tests. 2.3. Methods Freeze–thaw resistance was tested according to ASTM C666 and GB/T 50082- 2009 ‘‘Standard for test methods of long-term performance and durability of ordin- ary concrete’’. Six samples of each batch were tested, the average value of 6 samples was served as the ﬁnial freeze–thaw resistance. Mass and dynamic elasticity mod- ulus were tested once after an interval of 25 times cycles, maximal cycle times (when relative dynamic elasticity modulus was 60% and percentage of mass loss was 5% at lowest) can denote freeze–thaw resistance of ASC. TDR-16V computer controlled concrete fast freeze–thaw cycle testing machine and DT-10W dynamic elasticity modulus testing machine were used to conduct the tests. SEM and EDS were used to analyzed microstructure of ASC, tests were con- ducted in Xi’an 213 Institute of Chinese Enginery Industry Corporation. 3. Results and discussion 3.1. Freeze–thaw resistance durability of ASC Results of fast freeze–thaw cycle tests of ASC are listed in Table 3. As can be seen: (1) With the increase of freeze–thaw cycle times, relative dynamic elasticity modulus of ASC are descending slowly, this shows excellent ductility (which is shown in Fig. 1 ), relative dynamic elasticity modulus of A1–A5 are all about 90% at 300 times cycle, thereinto A5 has the best freeze–thaw resistance, its relative dynamic elasticity modulus is 91.49%; The percentage of mass of A1–A5 loss are very small, less than 1% at 300 times cycle, so ASC can meet the frost-resisting requirements in cold areas with frost-resisting grade of F300 at lowest. (2) It is improper to set mass loss of ASC as the evaluation index of freeze–thaw destroy, because mass loss of A1–A5 vary indistinctively in the progress of freeze–thaw, it cannot reﬂect the destroy degree of concrete exactly, thus it is improper to use it to test and evaluate the freeze–thaw damage of ASC (which is shown in Fig. 2 ). Especially the percentage of mass loss of A1 and A5 keep minus values before 200 times cycle, this can account for no denudation of concrete, then percentage of mass loss turn into plus values with the increasing of freeze–thaw cycles, that is to say, surface of

denudation of ASC samples, and surface damage layers of all sam- ples are very thin, thus ASC has excellent apparent performance. 3.2. Microstructure and freeze–thaw resistance mechanism of ASC Macroscopical performances of materials lie on its compositions and inner structure, SEM and EDS were used to analyze the micro- structure and performance mechanism of ASC, the results are given in Figs. 3 and 4 . As can be seen, ASC has little inner cracks, pores, and without transition strip. Lee and Van Deventer also founded that alkali-activated concrete did not have obvious interface tran- sition strip [13]. Hydration products of ASC are symmetrical and close-grained, cemented with surrounding binding products, and it does not have big and mass Ca(OH) 2 like PC, crystalloids com- plete and link with each other, all these can make excellent integer and close-grained properties of ASC, thus freeze–thaw resistance of ASC was improved. Hydration products of ASC have lots of O, Si, Ca, Al, Mg and Na elements, thereinto Si:Al = 3.46, Ca:Si = 1.03, and Ca/Si of C–S–H in PC is 1.5–1.7 [14], Ca/Si of C–S–H in alkali-slag cement is probably 1.1 [15], so C–S–H in ASC prepared in this work is C–S–H(I) with low Ca/Si and short, ordinal three-dimensional structure, and C–S–H(I) has better cushion power than C–S–H(II), which is advan- tageous of corrosion resistance of materials [16], makes water difﬁcult penetrate and difﬁcult for concrete become frozen and sat- urated, consequently freeze–thaw resistance of ASC was improved. Hydration products of ASC also have lots of alkaline aluminosil- icate and zeolite mineral except C–S–H, ﬁnal products are poly- meric aluminum–oxygen–silicon network structure products composed of [SiO 4]4/C0 and [AlO 4]5/C0 tetrahedrons synthesized by mutual oxygen, and without Ca(OH) 2 [15–19] , this is different from micro-ﬁber structure of PC [11]. Wang and Scrivener analyzed the chemic compositions of hydration products of ASC using EDS, and it were discovered that Al, Mg and Na distributed symmetrically in cement mortar, they compared the chemic compositions of sheet crystalloids, borderline and voluminous cement mortar, ﬁnally also proved these sheet crystalloids were C 4AH13 [18]. Close-grained hydration products and without transition strip make structure of ASC more compact, therefore, ASC has excellent freeze–thaw durability. Moreover, on condition that outer environment was unchang- ing, permeability resistance of concrete grew better with increas- ing of strength, and concrete of 80 MPa at lowest had enough high permeability resistance to resist the inﬁltration of water with- out air-entraining agent [20]. The compressive strength of ASC in this work were all above 86 MPa, which can improve the perme- ability resistance of ASC, accordingly effectively prevented water and Cl /C0 corrosive medium from inﬁltrating concrete, and greatly improved the freeze–thaw resistance of ASC. 4. Freeze–thaw damage mechanics models of ASC In the progress of freezing and thawing, recurrent frost expand- ing and penetration compressive stress act on ASC, each cycle pro- duced freeze–thaw inner stress in concrete interior, the stress made inner ﬂaws of concrete expand, accumulate and form new damage. As cycles are progressing, damage caused by freeze–thaw stress accumulate gradually, which can worsen the performances of ASC, this progress can be explained by rheology penetration theory and damage theory: no damage ? damage(micro-cracks form

Y1 ¼ 4:7197e0:0023N ; R2 ¼ 0:6855 Y2 ¼ 6:3579e0:0018N ; R2 ¼ 0:7277 Y3 ¼ 7:2858e0:0014N ; R2 ¼ 0:6617 Y4 ¼ 6:7982e0:0020N ; R2 ¼ 0:8202 Y5 ¼ 1:8183e0:0055N ; R2 ¼ 0:7125 Tt can be obviously observed from Figs. 7 and 8 and above equa- tions that freeze–thaw cycle cumulative damage models based on power function models are superior to exponential function mod- els with better precision, but all are inferior to precision of dy- namic elasticity modulus attenuation models. 5. Conclusions 1) ASC used industrial waste – slag as raw materials, and it had excellent freeze–thaw resistance with frost-resisting grade of F300 at lowest, relative dynamic elasticity modulus were about 90% after 300 times freeze–thaw cycles, it also had lit- tle mass loss, surface freeze–thaw damage layers were very thin, which can effectively restrain freeze–thaw damage of concrete from worsening. 2) Final hydration products of ASC were mostly C–S–H(I) with low Ca/Si, alkaline aluminosilicate and zeolite mineral, it did not have Ca(OH) 2 and transition strip, it is difﬁcult for water to penetrate due to its symmetrical and compact structure and difﬁcult for concrete to become frozen and sat- urated, and its high compressive strength also improved the ability to restrain interface damage from developing, conse- quently ASC had excellent freeze–thaw durability. 3) Different from freeze–thaw cycle damage models of PC, dynamic elasticity modulus attenuation models were supe- rior to accumulative freeze–thaw damage models, and power function models were superior to exponential func- tion models with better precision and relativity. 4) Dynamic elasticity modulus attenuation power function models can reﬂect freeze–thaw damage rule and degree of ASC accurately, so it can be used to evaluate and forecast freeze–thaw cycle standard life of ASC, this can establish research basic of quantiﬁcational design of freeze–thaw resistance and microcosmic study on ASC after freeze–thaw cycles reaction by mechanics methods. 5) Two freeze–thaw cycle damage models – dynamic elastic- ity modulus attenuation model and accumulative freeze– thaw damage model, were established based on tests results of freeze–thaw experiments, however, they still need amending and perfecting owing to difference of opin- ions of choosing freeze–thaw damage variable. Further- more there are many factors affecting freeze–thaw resistance of ASC, it is not enough to investigate at the aspects of experiments and materials science, and freeze–thaw durability of ASC is still required to be inves- tigated in cross ﬁeld of materials, structures and mechanics. Dynamic elasticity modulus attenuation models Supposed E0 as dynamic elasticity modulus of concrete without damage, En as remnant dynamic elasticity modulus after N times freeze–thaw cycles, attenuation velocity as dE/dN which was direct proportion to attenuation quantity of dynamic elasticity modulus at (0– N) time, exponential function equations were established based on dynamic elasticity modulus attenuation model in litera- ture [22]: Y ¼ En=E0 ¼ aebN ð1Þ Ei ¼ E0aebN ð2Þ As can be seen from Eq. (2), unbreakage of concrete structure is attenuating with integrality of original structure at natural rate, this is the theoretic attenuation equation, which accord with Isaac Newton’s ‘‘matter cooling law’’. According to Eq. (1) and testing results in Table 3 , relative dy- namic elasticity modulus attenuation model can be established as below (which is shown in Fig. 5 ): Y1 ¼ 95:308e/C0 0:0002N ; R2 ¼ 0:8055 Y2 ¼ 93:668e/C0 0:0001N ; R2 ¼ 0:8078 Y3 ¼ 92:683e/C0 0:0001N ; R2 ¼ 0:7036 Y4 ¼ 93:314e/C0 0:0002N ; R2 ¼ 0:8638 Y5 ¼ 98:438e/C0 0:0002N ; R2 ¼ 0:8660 At the same time, According to power function Eq. (3) and test- ing results in Table 3 relative dynamic elasticity modulus attenua- tion model can be established as below (which is shown in Fig. 6): Y ¼ Ei=E0 ¼ aNb ð3Þ Y1 ¼ 102:47N/C0 0:0201; R2 ¼ 0:9277 Y2 ¼ 100:90N/C0 0:0205; R2 ¼ 0:9624 Y3 ¼ 98:863N/C0 0:0175; R2 ¼ 0:9061 Y4 ¼ 101:73N/C0 0:0240; R2 ¼ 0:9624 Y5 ¼ 108:25N/C0 0:0268; R2 ¼ 0:9009 Tt can be observed from Figs. 5 and 6 and above equations that dynamic elasticity modulus attenuation exponential function models are not precise enough, and have bigger forecast error, however, power function models have enough precision. Hereto- fore freeze–thaw damage models of PC were mostly exponential function models, power function models were used rarely before [21,22]. This work can show that, as to ASC, power function models are superior to exponential function models with better precision and relativity. 4.2. Freeze–thaw cycle cumulative damage model models Damage degree of concrete after freeze–thaw cycles can be de- ﬁned as Eq. (4) based on basal theory of damage mechanics, that is to say, relative dynamic elasticity modulus are tokens of damage degree of concrete (damage variable). Damage degree increases with the reducing of relative dynamic elasticity modulus of con- crete after freeze–thaw cycles: D ¼ 1 /C0 EiE/C0 1 0 ð4Þ Based on above deﬁnition, degree of freeze–thaw cycle cumula- tive damage is functions of times of freeze–thaw cycles, and dam- age models of concrete can be established based on power function Eq. (5) and exponential function Eq.
